_meta.horizon: FY2029 (the 5-year guidance window 2024-2029), with a normalization read to ~2030

crux.variable: The SIZE and DURABILITY of TSMC's leading-edge / HPC revenue engine through ~2029-2030, expressed as the whole-company USD revenue CAGR 2024-2029 — which is governed by (a) the AI/HPC demand POOL and (b) TSMC's pricing power on a sold-out leading edge. NOT competitive share (already maxed) but pool-size x ASP.
crux.one_line: TSMC keeps ~all of leading-edge foundry no matter what; the call is how big the AI/HPC wafer pool gets and how hard TSMC can price it — i.e. whether the 25% USD revenue CAGR guide proves conservative, met, or optimistic.
crux.why_this_is_the_crux: Share is settled: TSMC holds 90%+ of <7nm and effectively 100% of merchant AI-accelerator manufacturing, and grew ~4x faster than rivals in 2025. So the variance in TSM's value over the horizon comes almost entirely from one place — the dollar size of the leading-edge/HPC revenue line, which is the product of the AI demand pool (set by hyperscaler + sovereign AI capex) and TSMC's price per wafer. Margin and the mature segments are second-order; the bull and bear cases are different honest readings of how big and durable the AI/HPC pool is and how much pricing TSMC extracts from it.
crux.decomposition_identity: Company revenue CAGR ~= weighted( AI-accelerator revenue CAGR x AI-weight ) + ( rest-of-HPC CAGR x weight ) + ( smartphone CAGR x weight ) + ( IoT/auto/other CAGR x weight ). The swing term is AI-accelerator revenue = (AI accelerator unit/wafer demand, set by data-center capex) x (TSMC ASP per wafer, set by node + packaging pricing power). AI accelerator was ~17-19% of company revenue in 2025 (~$21-23B) and is the fastest-growing term; HPC overall is 61% of revenue.

drivers.driver: AI accelerator demand pool (the dominant term)
drivers.current: AI-accelerator revenue ~17-19% of TSMC revenue in 2025 (~$21-23B), roughly doubled YoY. The end-pool: data-center AI accelerator TAM ~$300B+ in 2025 heading to >$500B by 2028 (AMD), with bull houses >$1T/yr by 2030.
drivers.trajectory_sourced: TSMC raised its AI-accelerator revenue CAGR guide to 54-56% for 2024-2029 (1Q26 call), up from mid-40s a year earlier and mid-to-high 50s at 4Q25. CC Wei called AI demand 'extremely robust'. N2 sold out through 2Q27; CoWoS scaling ~35k->120-130k wpm by end-26; Nvidia alone secured >60% of 2026 CoWoS.
drivers.evidence_class: CONTRACTED near term (sold-out N2, booked CoWoS, FY26 guide >30% USD), EXTRAPOLATED out-years (the 54-56% CAGR rests on hyperscaler+sovereign capex continuing).

sources.value: AI accel CAGR 54-56% 2024-29 (1Q26); ~17-19% of 2025 rev (~$21-23B), ~doubled YoY

drivers.trajectory_sourced: Management frames leading-edge price increases as 'unavoidable' to hold 53%+ GM, and raised the long-term GM target to '56% and higher'. With no second source at the leading edge, hikes pass through with little pushback ('effective monopoly pricing on 2nm from now through end-2027').
drivers.evidence_class: OBSERVABLE — pricing already set for N2 and 2026; durable because of structural single-source position.

sources.value: Effective monopoly pricing on 2nm through end-2027; 1Q26 GM 66.2%

drivers.driver: Competitive share (the term that is NOT contested)
drivers.current: TSMC 72.3% pure-play foundry (1Q26, up from 70.4%), >90% leading edge, ~100% merchant AI accelerators.
drivers.trajectory_sourced: Samsung ~11x smaller and yield-behind; SMIC walled off by export controls; only Intel 18A/14A is a credible challenger and it is unprofitable with yields-to-parity not before ~2027 and no proven external AI wins. Share flows toward TSMC.
drivers.evidence_class: OBSERVABLE and stable — this is why share is NOT the crux; it bounds the downside.

sources.value: TSMC 72.3% foundry share 1Q26; grew 4x faster than rivals 2025

crux.ceiling: Set by the AI/data-center demand pool, not by TSMC's share. If data-center AI accelerator TAM reaches ~$500B by 2028 and TSMC manufactures effectively all of it (merchant + ASIC), the wafer+packaging revenue TSMC captures from AI alone scales toward the high-$60s-$80B+ range by ~2029 on the company's own 54-56% CAGR. The physical ceiling is hyperscaler+sovereign capex sustaining (the 'is AI capex durable' question) and TSMC's own capacity (N2/A16 fabs + CoWoS), which TSMC is funding at $52-56B/yr.
crux.floor: Bounded by the share moat and the contracted book. Even in a sharp AI digestion, TSMC keeps ~all leading-edge share, smartphone/IoT/auto provide a ~$45-50B annuity-ish base, and the near term is contracted (N2 sold out to 2Q27, FY26 guide >30% USD). The floor is a slowdown in the AI pool, not a loss of position.

base.estimate: Whole-company USD revenue CAGR ~24-26% 2024-2029 (i.e. TSMC's own 25% guide is the honest base, treated as a credible management commitment, not a sandbag). AI-accelerator revenue CAGR ~50-56% within that. Blended gross margin holds ~57-59% (mid-to-high 50s) net of overseas dilution.
base.argument: TSMC's 25% USD CAGR guide is itself a raised number (from 20% a year ago) and management has a long record of meeting or beating its long-term frame; the near term is contracted (N2 sold out to 2Q27, FY26 guided >30%, 1Q26 already +6.4% QoQ at 66.2% GM). The AI-accelerator 54-56% CAGR is anchored on real booked capacity (CoWoS scaling 3-4x, Nvidia locking 60% of 2026 packaging) and a demand pool independently forecast at >$500B by 2028. Pricing is set and durable (N2 +10-20%, single-source). The honest most-likely is that the guide is roughly right: AI/HPC compounds in the low-50s%, the leading-edge pool is real, and TSMC prices it well — landing the company at a ~25% top-line CAGR with mid-to-high-50s margins. This is neither a sandbag nor heroics; it is management's contracted+extrapolated frame taken at face value because the contracted portion validates it.

bull.estimate: Whole-company USD revenue CAGR ~28-32% 2024-2029; AI-accelerator CAGR ~60%+; gross margin sustaining 59-62%.
bull.argument: The credible higher reading: AI capex proves a multi-year megatrend rather than a cycle — sovereign AI, inference scaling, and agentic/physical AI push the data-center accelerator TAM toward the >$1T/yr-by-2030 forecasts. TSMC, as the sole manufacturer of effectively all AI silicon AND the binding packaging constraint (CoWoS), captures the upside on both volume and price: it raises ASP harder (single-source on N2/A16), CoWoS becomes a fat second revenue line, and overseas-fab pricing premiums (US customers paying for Arizona supply security) lift the blend. In this world the 25% guide proves conservative and TSMC runs in the high-20s/low-30s with margins above target. Evidence class: EXTRAPOLATED-to-HOPED-FOR on the out-year capex durability, but grounded in observable booked capacity and pricing today.

bear.estimate: Whole-company USD revenue CAGR ~14-18% 2024-2029; AI-accelerator CAGR decelerates to ~30-40%; gross margin drifts to low-to-mid 50s.
bear.argument: The credible lower reading: AI accelerator demand digests after a 2024-2027 build-out — hyperscaler capex growth normalizes, some inference shifts to cheaper/in-house silicon, and a 2027-2028 air-pocket hits leading-edge utilization (the classic semiconductor cycle, which TSMC is not immune to). Pricing power stays but on a smaller volume; overseas-fab dilution (Arizona/Japan/Germany, ~2-4 ppt) and TWD strength compress the blend toward low-50s GM; FX and a high capex base pressure FCF. Note this is a DEMAND-pool bear, not a share bear — even here TSMC keeps its position; the line just grows slower than the guide. A more severe (low-probability) tail is geopolitical disruption to Taiwan, which is a binary, not a fade. Evidence class: MECHANISM (capex cyclicality is real and observable in semi history) for the ~15% read; TAIL for the geopolitical leg.

what_decides_it: Hyperscaler + sovereign AI capex durability through 2027-2029 (the demand pool) — watch the big-4 hyperscaler capex guides and Nvidia/AMD/ASIC order books.
CoWoS / advanced-packaging capacity ramp actually filling (35k->120-130k wpm) and staying booked — the binding constraint and the tell on real demand.
TSMC's ability to sustain leading-edge price increases (N2/A16 ASP) without demand destruction — the pricing-power leg of the crux.
Margin defense vs overseas-fab dilution and TWD — whether the 56%+ GM target holds as Arizona/Japan/Germany scale.
Intel 18A/14A external traction — the ONLY thing that could touch the share floor; watch for a marquee AI/fabless dual-source win.
